settings.title: Your Cold-Snap Checklist for Frozen Pipes
settings.excerpt: A short list of jobs to do before the first hard frost, while it is still easy to do them.
settings.body:
The coldest week of the year is when our phones never stop, and most of those calls were preventable in autumn. Here is what we tell our own families to do, while it is still mild enough to do it comfortably.

Before the first hard frost

  • Lag any pipe in a loft, garage, or against an outside wall.
  • Know where your stopcock is and check it still turns.
  • Leave the heating ticking over low if you go away in a frost.
  • Disconnect and drain garden hoses and outside taps.

The pipes that freeze are almost always the ones in unheated spaces. A loft, a garage, or the short run to an outside tap sees none of your central heating, so a few pounds of foam lagging on those is the single best thing you can do. Pay particular attention to any pipe near a draughty vent or airbrick.

If you are heading away in a cold spell, set the heating to come on low rather than switching it off entirely. A house left to go stone cold is exactly where pipes burst, and the running cost of a low background heat is a fraction of an emergency repair.

If a pipe does freeze

Turn off the water and thaw it gently with a warm cloth or a hairdryer on low, never a flame. A burst on thawing is the usual culprit behind a flooded kitchen, because the split happens silently in the ice and only shows itself once the water flows again. Thaw slowly and keep an eye on it.

A quick lagging job in November is a fraction of the cost of a January emergency. If you would rather we did the rounds for you, we can lag the awkward runs and check your stopcock in a single short visit.
